name: God of Mummification | Egyptian Mythology
shortDescription: Anubis is the ancient Egyptian god of mummification, afterlife, and cemeteries, often depicted with the head of a jackal.
description: Anubis, known in ancient Egyptian as Inpu, is a prominent deity associated with death, mummification, embalming, the afterlife, cemeteries, tombs, and the Underworld. He is typically depicted as a man with the head of a jackal or in full jackal form, a representation likely stemming from the jackals that frequented cemeteries in ancient Egypt. His role was crucial in the process of mummification, where he oversaw the weighing of the heart ceremony in the Hall of Ma'at, determining whether the deceased was worthy to enter the afterlife. The heart was weighed against the feather of Ma'at (truth and justice). If the heart was lighter, the deceased could pass on to the realm of Osiris; if heavier, they would be devoured by Ammit. Anubis also guided souls through the Duat (the underworld). Although his parentage varies in different accounts, he is commonly believed to be the son of Nephthys and either Set or Osiris. With the rise of Osiris' cult, Anubis' role as ruler of the underworld gradually diminished, and he became more of a guide and protector of the dead, assisting Osiris in his judgment. Anubis was highly revered and played a central role in funerary rituals and beliefs throughout ancient Egyptian history.
epithets: He Who is Upon His Mountain

sources.title: The Egyptian Book of the Dead
sources.author: Translated by E.A. Wallis Budge

sources.title: The Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt
sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson

sources.title: Death and Salvation in Ancient Egypt
sources.author: Jan Assmann

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of food, incense, and linen were commonly presented to Anubis at temples and tombs. These offerings were meant to honor the god and ensure safe passage to the afterlife for the deceased.

worshipPractices.name: Prayers and Incantations
worshipPractices.description: Prayers and incantations were recited to Anubis, often by priests during the mummification process. These rituals sought his protection and guidance for the deceased in the afterlife, ensuring their successful transition.

festivals.name: Khoiak Festival
festivals.timing: Annually, spanning several days in the Egyptian month of Khoiak (roughly October-November)
festivals.description: While dedicated to Osiris, Anubis played a crucial role in the festival as the embalmer who prepared Osiris's body for resurrection. Rituals would include recreating aspects of the mummification process and processions honoring Osiris and Anubis.

cultural.modernLegacy: Anubis remains a recognizable symbol of ancient Egypt, frequently appearing in movies, books, and video games. He is often depicted as a powerful and mysterious figure, representing the allure and intrigue of ancient Egyptian funerary practices and beliefs about the afterlife.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Thanatos is the personification of death in Greek mythology. While not specifically tied to mummification, he guides souls to the underworld and is a figure associated with the end of life.

Mictlantecuhtli is the Aztec god of the dead and the ruler of Mictlan, the underworld. He is connected with death rituals and the journey of souls after death, sharing the association with the afterlife.
Yama is the Hindu god of death and justice. He judges the souls of the dead and determines their fate in the afterlife, highlighting a similar role in the realm of death and the deceased.
Nergal is a Mesopotamian god associated with death, plague, war, and the underworld. His dominion over the dead and the underworld establishes a parallel with Anubis's connection to the afterlife.

associations.name: Jackal
associations.significance: Jackals were scavengers that frequented cemeteries, making them a natural symbol for a deity associated with death and the afterlife.

associations.name: Black
associations.significance: Black represents the fertile soil of the Nile and the regenerative properties associated with death and rebirth.

associations.name: Immortality
associations.significance: Mummification was performed to preserve the body for the afterlife, symbolizing the pursuit of eternal life.

associations.name: Sand
associations.significance: Sand, prevalent in the Egyptian desert, represents the environment where tombs and cemeteries were often located.
Associated with the cardinal directions and completeness, reflecting Anubis's role in guiding souls through the afterlife.

associations.name: Sirius
associations.significance: The rising of Sirius was associated with the Nile's flood, which brought fertility and renewal, paralleling the concept of rebirth after death.

associations.name: Myrrh
associations.significance: Myrrh was a key ingredient in the embalming process, thus linking it to Anubis and the preservation of the body.

companions.name: Thoth
companions.description: Thoth, the god of wisdom and writing, often assists Anubis in the Weighing of the Heart ceremony, recording the results and ensuring accurate judgment of the deceased's soul.

companions.name: Bastet
companions.description: Bastet, as a protector of the home and family, is sometimes associated with guarding tombs and cemeteries, working alongside Anubis to safeguard the deceased.

companions.name: Embalmers
companions.description: The embalmers, priests dedicated to Anubis, perform the sacred rituals of mummification, preparing the deceased for their journey into the afterlife under Anubis's guidance.

companions.name: Weighing Scale
companions.description: The scales used in the Weighing of the Heart ceremony are a sacred item used during judging the deceased's soul, with Anubis and Thoth using it to balance Ma'at against the heart.

companions.name: Ma'at
companions.description: Ma'at, the goddess of truth and justice, her feather used in the Weighing of the Heart ceremony, where Anubis oversees the judgment of the deceased's soul and their worthiness to enter the afterlife.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content:
Anubis's origins are shrouded in some mythological ambiguity, with different accounts of his parentage. In earlier myths, he was considered a son of Ra and Hesat, a cow goddess associated with nourishment. However, the most prevalent narrative designates him as the offspring of Nephthys and Osiris. This version, steeped in intrigue, portrays Nephthys, sister and wife of Set, disguising herself as Isis to seduce Osiris. Their union resulted in Anubis, who Nephthys abandoned out of fear of Set's wrath. Isis, upon discovering the infant, adopted and raised him as her own. This narrative establishes Anubis's complex familial ties and implicitly links him to both the divine order (Osiris) and the potential for disruption (Set). 

The circumstances of his birth also highlight themes of deception, hidden identity, and the power of maternal bonds. Isis's role in protecting and nurturing Anubis further elevates her status as a maternal figure and underscores her dedication to upholding cosmic balance. The initial abandonment by Nephthys adds a layer of complexity to his character, possibly contributing to his later role as a protector of the deceased and a guide through the perils of the afterlife. He is also considered a protector to orphaned souls, and thus the orphaned Anubis' beginnings gave him this reputation.

The uncertainty surrounding Anubis's parentage may also reflect the evolving nature of Egyptian religious beliefs over time. As the Osiris myth gained prominence, Anubis's role became increasingly intertwined with the cycle of death and resurrection associated with Osiris. Regardless of the specific account, Anubis's origins firmly establish him as a powerful and significant figure within the Egyptian pantheon, deeply connected to the mysteries of life, death, and the afterlife.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Temples
extendedContent.content:
While Anubis did not have numerous large-scale temples dedicated solely to him, his presence was ubiquitous throughout Egypt, particularly in mortuary contexts. His worship was deeply interwoven with the funerary rituals and the cult of the dead. Priests wearing Anubis masks officiated at embalming ceremonies, embodying the god's role in preparing the deceased for their journey to the afterlife. Evidence of his worship is found in numerous tombs and cemeteries across Egypt, from the Old Kingdom onward. In the ancient city of Cynopolis, meaning “city of the dog,” Anubis was the main deity, and the city's inhabitants revered him, and many dogs were buried there to honor the jackal god. 

Specific chapels and shrines dedicated to Anubis were often located within or near cemeteries and embalming workshops. These served as focal points for prayers, offerings, and rituals intended to ensure the safe passage of the deceased into the next world. Inscriptions and artwork found in these locations frequently depict Anubis guiding souls through the underworld and weighing their hearts against the feather of Ma'at. The presence of Anubis figurines and amulets in tombs further underscores the personal devotion to the god and the belief in his protective powers.

The most complete, extant temple dedicated to Anubis is found in the temple complex of Hatshepsut at Deir el-Bahri. This chapel is dedicated to Anubis, and contains offering scenes, and depictions of the Pharoah Hatshepsut making offerings to Anubis. The walls are painted in great detail and color, and are a true testament to the worship of Anubis. While not as grand as temples dedicated to other major deities like Ra or Amun, the consistent presence of Anubis in mortuary contexts and the existence of dedicated shrines demonstrate the profound importance of his worship in ancient Egyptian society. He was a constant presence in the lives of the Egyptians, specifically in the events related to the afterlife.

extendedContent.title: Iconography & Representation
extendedContent.content:
Anubis is most famously depicted as a jackal-headed man, a representation directly linked to the jackals that scavenged in cemeteries. The jackal's association with death and decay made it a fitting symbol for the god responsible for protecting the deceased. He is typically shown with black fur, not to represent the actual color of jackals (which are usually brown or tan), but as a symbol of the fertile black soil of the Nile Valley, representing rebirth and regeneration. In some depictions, Anubis is portrayed in full jackal form, emphasizing his animalistic nature and connection to the wild. 

In human form, Anubis is often shown wearing traditional Egyptian clothing, including a kilt and a nemes headdress or wig. He carries the ankh, the symbol of life, and the was-scepter, representing power and dominion. These attributes reinforce his divine status and his authority over the realm of the dead. The combination of human and animal features in Anubis's iconography reflects the Egyptian belief in the interconnectedness of the human and animal worlds, and the ability of deities to manifest in both forms.

Beyond the standard jackal-headed representation, Anubis can also be depicted holding scales, referencing his role in the Weighing of the Heart ceremony. In this scene, the heart of the deceased is placed on one side of the scale, while the feather of Ma'at (truth and justice) is placed on the other. Anubis oversees this process, ensuring a fair judgment. If the heart is lighter than the feather, the deceased is deemed worthy to enter the afterlife. If the heart is heavier, it is devoured by Ammit, the Devourer of Souls, resulting in the soul's eternal destruction. This visual depiction of Anubis as the overseer of judgment solidified his role as the guardian of moral order in the afterlife.

extendedContent.title: Role in Cosmic Order
extendedContent.content:
Anubis's role extended beyond the practical aspects of mummification and funerary rites; he was a crucial figure in maintaining cosmic order (Ma'at) within the context of death and the afterlife. By ensuring the proper preparation of the deceased and guiding them through the perils of the underworld, Anubis helped prevent chaos from spilling over into the realm of the living. His role in the Weighing of the Heart ceremony was particularly significant in upholding Ma'at, as it determined whether a soul was worthy of eternal life based on their moral conduct during their earthly existence.

As a guardian of tombs and cemeteries, Anubis protected the deceased from desecration and ensured that their remains were treated with respect. This was essential for maintaining the integrity of the afterlife and preventing the disruption of the cycle of death and rebirth. Anubis's presence served as a deterrent to tomb robbers and other malevolent forces that might seek to disturb the sacred space of the dead. The Egyptians believed that disturbing the dead could bring about chaos and misfortune in the world of the living, so Anubis's protective role was vital for maintaining the balance between the two realms.

Furthermore, Anubis's association with Osiris and the resurrection myth reinforced his connection to the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth. Osiris's resurrection, facilitated by Anubis's embalming skills, symbolized the triumph of order over chaos and the promise of eternal life for those who lived righteously. Anubis's role in this process solidified his position as a key figure in the Egyptian understanding of the cosmos and the hope for a positive afterlife. His meticulous attention to detail and his unwavering commitment to upholding Ma'at made him an indispensable part of the divine order.
